one_line_takeaway: Chat-template special-token boundaries are an architectural (not semantic) attack surface: an automated latent-space Bayesian-optimization search over structured 'ghost history' templates (Phantom) achieves 79.76% average ASR across seven closed-source agents plus DeepSeek, evades semantic-alignment and filter-based defenses far better than prompt-based baselines, and found 70 confirmed real-world vulnerabilities including a CVE in MCP-integrated open-source agent frameworks.
key_finding: On AgentDojo, Phantom achieves an aggregate average ASR of 79.76% across the eight agents, versus 54.09% (Single-Template), 39.86% (Semantic-Injection), and 38.46% (ChatInject). Per-model/per-defense results from Table 2 (Workspace scenario, w/o defense vs. under defenses): on GPT-4.1, Phantom sustains 75.97% ASR undefended and 76.62% under Delimiter Spotlighting while Semantic-Injection collapses from 14.29% to 12.99%; under the Tag Filter, Phantom achieves 50.00% ASR on Qwen-Flash versus roughly 9.74% for Semantic-Injection (a >40-point gap); the Injection Detector suppresses ASR substantially (e.g., Phantom to 12.34% on GPT-4o) but Table 3 shows this comes at a severe utility cost (utility falls to 15.58% for GPT-4o under the Injection Detector). The paper reports a 'capability curse': newer/more capable models (e.g., GPT-4.1 vs. GPT-4o; Gemini-3-Flash vs. Gemini-2.5-Flash) are more, not less, vulnerable to structural injection despite improved resistance to semantic injection, because stronger instruction-following amplifies faithful execution of the forged conversation history. The attention-perturbation study (Table 4) shows attack success (probability of the target tool call) drops from 100.00% (original template) to 0.05% (bracket removal) and 0.00% (full template removal), while token-level obfuscations that preserve tag-style syntax (Force Tokenization: 99.99%; HTML Bracket Encoding: 46.29%) largely retain potency -- indicating the exploit depends on preserved bracket/delimiter syntactic structure, not on exact special-token identifiers. In the real-world deployment, Phantom identified 70 distinct confirmed vulnerabilities across 942 commercial agents (arbitrary command execution, resource exhaustion via recursive tool invocation, unauthorized data exfiltration), uncovered a systemic MCP-integration flaw in OpenHands/AutoGen assigned CVE-2025-6***4, and achieved privilege escalation to full cloud-desktop control against Agentbay via a comment-section web injection.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Phantom, an automated black-box agent-hijacking attack framework that exploits chat-template parsing (rather than semantic persuasion) to induce 'role confusion' in LLM agents. Phantom (1) uses multi-level template augmentation (LLM-driven semantic rewriting of 78 canonical templates plus a six-category rule-based character-level mutation engine with Bernoulli trigger probability p=0.1) to build a large seed pool of structured injection templates; (2) trains a Template Autoencoder (TAE, built on a LoRA-fine-tuned Qwen3-0.6B backbone) that embeds discrete templates -- serialized as a triplet (T0: deceptive tool-call termination, T1: forged assistant response, T2: forged user query) -- into a continuous, differentiable latent space; and (3) runs Bayesian optimization (Random Forest surrogate + Expected Improvement acquisition) over that latent space, guided by a lightweight proxy evaluation (a round-counting dialogue-state-consistency test, Algorithm 2) that avoids costly/risky direct attacks on production agents during search. The decoded optimal template is injected into retrieved content (web pages, emails, tool responses) to hijack agent execution.
threat_model: Remote, black-box adversary who can inject arbitrary content into data sources the target agent retrieves (web pages, emails, API responses) but has no access to model weights, gradients, internal system prompts, execution traces, or the specific chat-template format in use. The attacker relies only on query access to iteratively refine payloads based on observable outputs. The attack targets the agent's structural/architectural parsing logic (chat-template token boundaries such as <|im_start|>/<|im_end|>) rather than its semantic alignment, aiming to make the agent misclassify injected text as legitimate prior 'Assistant' output or a new 'User' instruction, bypassing safety alignment and context-filtering defenses. Success is formalized as A(q, x_adv) = o_adv where o_adv satisfies an adversarial goal g_adv distinct from the user's goal g_user.
limitations: Paper's own stated limitations (Section 7, Discussion): the study is restricted to text-based interactions and does not address multimodal (vision/audio) agent attack surfaces; the black-box threat model assumes no prior knowledge of target template specifications, so an insider with training-data access could build more potent payloads; the iterative-query optimization approach may struggle against systems with strict rate limiting or anomaly detection; and the evaluation metrics measure only immediate hijacking success, not long-term persistence within complex conversational state-management systems.

datasets_used: AgentDojo benchmark (Workspace, Travel, Slack, Banking domains; 97 legitimate user tasks, 629 adversarial test cases)
78 canonical chat templates collected via crawling/manual verification (seed corpus)
3,833 semantically distinct templates after LLM-based semantic-level augmentation and dedup
~18,000 training templates and 2,000 validation templates for TAE training
942 commercial-grade agents from a partner enterprise's production infrastructure (real-world evaluation)
OpenHands (67.4k stars) and AutoGen (54.2k stars) open-source agent frameworks
quantitative_results: Aggregate average ASR on AgentDojo: Phantom 79.76% vs Single-Template 54.09% vs Semantic-Injection 39.86% vs ChatInject 38.46%
GPT-4.1, Workspace scenario, w/o defense: Phantom 75.97% ASR vs Semantic-Injection 14.29%
GPT-4.1, Workspace, under Delimiter Spotlighting: Phantom 76.62% ASR vs Semantic-Injection 12.99% ASR
Qwen-Flash, Workspace, under Tag Filter: Phantom 50.00% ASR vs Semantic-Injection 9.74% ASR (baseline ASR under Tag Filter from Table 2)
GPT-4o utility under Injection Detector defense drops to 15.58% while Phantom ASR is suppressed to 12.34%
70 confirmed vulnerabilities found across 942 commercial agents in real-world deployment
1 CVE assigned (CVE-2025-6***4) for a systemic MCP-integration flaw in OpenHands/AutoGen

Attention/perturbation study (Qwen3-8B): target tool-call probability 100.00% (original template) -> 99.99% (Force Tokenization) -> 46.29% (HTML Bracket Encoding) -> 0.05% (Bracket Removal) -> 0.00% (Full Template Removal)

TAE latent-dimension ablation (Table 5): at dim=16, Exact Match 87.26%/F1 97.95% with ASR 55.84% (Qwen-Flash) and 78.57% (DeepSeek-V3.2); ASR peaks at intermediate latent dimensions rather than scaling monotonically with reconstruction fidelity
Gemini-3-Flash ASR rises from 58.4% to 68.2% within the first 40 Bayesian-optimization iterations before plateauing (Search Iterations analysis)
baselines_compared: Semantic-Injection (AgentDojo's best-performing indirect prompt injection baseline, using <INFORMATION> delimiter tags + Perez et al. instruction-ignoring strategy)
Single-Template (static official Qwen3/GPT-OSS/Gemma3 chat templates used as structural proxies against closed-source counterparts)
ChatInject (Chang et al. 2025, prior chat-template-abuse structural injection attack)
